name: Thallium
name_origin: From Greek 'thallos' meaning 'green shoot' or 'green twig', after the brilliant green emission line discovered by William Crookes in 1861 when he examined a residue from sulfuric acid manufacture spectroscopically.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Thallium is rare in Earth's crust at about 0.85 ppm. It does not form major ores; it is dispersed in potassium-bearing minerals such as feldspars and micas, and concentrated in trace amounts in sulfide ores of copper, lead, and zinc. Most production is as a byproduct of zinc and lead smelting and the roasting of pyrite for sulfuric acid.
states: bound in crookesite, lorandite (TlAsS2) and hutchinsonite
trace in sulfide ores of Cu, Pb, Zn
byproduct of zinc roasting and pyrite processing

compounds.name: Thallium(I) sulfate
compounds.summary: A colorless, water-soluble salt used historically as an odorless and tasteless rodenticide and ant poison until banned in many countries.

compounds.name: Thallium(I) bromide
compounds.summary: A pale yellow solid used in infrared optics (with TlI as KRS-5/KRS-6 salts) for windows and prisms transparent in the mid- and far-IR.

compounds.name: Thallium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: A black oxide used in some high-temperature superconductor formulations such as the Tl-Ba-Ca-Cu-O family.

compounds.name: Thallium(I) hydroxide
compounds.summary: A strong base, more soluble than most heavy-metal hydroxides, with chemistry resembling that of an alkali metal hydroxide.

compounds.name: Thallium(III) nitrate
compounds.summary: A reagent used in organic synthesis for oxidative ring contractions and aromatic thallation.

uses: Highly sensitive infrared detectors and IR-transparent optics (TlBr, TlI mixtures known as KRS-5/KRS-6)
Myocardial perfusion imaging using 201Tl-thallous chloride in nuclear cardiology
Doping component in some high-Tc copper-oxide superconductors
Photoelectric cells and gamma-ray scintillation detectors (NaI(Tl), CsI(Tl))
Low-temperature thermometers (Hg-Tl alloys remain liquid below the freezing point of pure Hg)
Specialty optical glasses with high refractive index
reactivity_summary: Thallium is a soft, malleable, gray post-transition metal that tarnishes rapidly in moist air to form a bluish-gray oxide layer and reacts with steam to liberate hydrogen. It dissolves slowly in dilute mineral acids, more rapidly in HNO3. The +1 oxidation state is dominant and resembles alkali-metal chemistry, while +3 compounds are strongly oxidizing. All thallium compounds are highly toxic by ingestion, inhalation, or skin contact and act as cumulative poisons that mimic potassium uptake.
isotopes_summary: Thallium has two naturally occurring stable isotopes: 203Tl (~29.5%) and 205Tl (~70.5%). Several radioisotopes are members of the natural decay chains (208Tl, 210Tl). The synthetic isotope 201Tl (t1/2 ~73 h) is widely used in nuclear-cardiology stress-perfusion imaging because Tl+ behaves analogously to K+ and is taken up by viable myocardial cells.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
